title.en: Descriptors: The Magic Behind @property, Methods, and Slots

description.en: A descriptor is any object that defines __get__, __set__, or __delete__ and lives as a CLASS attribute — it lets that class hook into attribute access. This single protocol secretly powers @property, bound methods, classmethod, staticmethod, and __slots__. Learn data vs non-data descriptors, their precedence against the instance __dict__, and how attribute lookup actually resolves.

story_variants.en:
## The Protocol Hiding in Plain Sight

You have used descriptors a hundred times without naming them. Every time you wrote `@property`, called a method, used `@classmethod`, or added `__slots__`, the descriptor protocol did the work. It is the single most important — and most invisible — mechanism in Python's object model.

The definition is almost insultingly small. **A descriptor is any object that defines at least one of `__get__`, `__set__`, or `__delete__`, and is stored as a *class* attribute.** That's it. When you then access that attribute *through an instance*, Python doesn't hand you the descriptor object — it calls the descriptor's methods and hands you whatever they return.

```python
class Descriptor:
    def __get__(self, obj, objtype=None):
        return "intercepted!"

class Thing:
    x = Descriptor()      # stored on the CLASS

print(Thing().x)          # 'intercepted!' — __get__ ran
```

> **Key idea:** A descriptor customizes what `obj.attr` *does*. Storing it as a class attribute turns ordinary attribute access into a method call you control.

## Two Flavors: Data vs Non-Data

The protocol splits into two kinds, and the distinction governs everything about precedence.

| | Data descriptor | Non-data descriptor |
|---|---|---|
| Defines | `__set__` and/or `__delete__` (usually with `__get__`) | only `__get__` |
| Examples | `property`, `__slots__` members | functions (methods), `classmethod`, `staticmethod` |
| Beats instance `__dict__`? | **Yes** — wins over per-instance data | **No** — instance `__dict__` wins |
| Typical use | validated/typed fields, computed-and-settable values | binding `self`, lazy/cached values |

The presence of `__set__` (or `__delete__`) is the *only* thing that makes a descriptor a "data" descriptor. That one method flips its priority during lookup.

## How Attribute Lookup *Actually* Works

When you evaluate `obj.attr`, CPython's `type(obj).__getattribute__` runs a precise algorithm. Walk it once and you can predict any result:

1. Search `type(obj).__mro__` for `attr`. If found and it's a **data descriptor**, call its `__get__` and **return immediately**. (Data descriptors win first.)
2. Otherwise, look in the instance's own `obj.__dict__`. If `attr` is there, return that value.
3. Otherwise, fall back to the class attribute found in step 1. If it's a **non-data descriptor**, call its `__get__`; if it's a plain value, return it as-is.
4. If nothing matched, raise `AttributeError` (after trying `__getattr__`).

The order is the whole story: **data descriptor → instance dict → non-data descriptor → AttributeError.** This is why a `@property` (data descriptor) can't be shadowed by setting `obj.name = ...`, but a method (non-data descriptor) *can* be overridden by an instance attribute of the same name.

## Why Methods Work At All

A plain function is a non-data descriptor: it defines `__get__`. When you write `obj.method`, the function's `__get__(obj, type)` runs and returns a **bound method** — a small wrapper that remembers `obj` and forwards it as `self`. That is literally how `self` gets passed. `staticmethod.__get__` returns the underlying function unchanged (no binding); `classmethod.__get__` binds the *class* instead of the instance. Three lines of behavior, one protocol.

And `__slots__`? Each slot name becomes a data descriptor on the class that reads and writes a fixed memory offset instead of a per-instance `__dict__`. That's why slotted instances have no `__dict__` and reject unknown attributes — the descriptors are the *only* storage.

## A Real Use: Validated, Typed Attributes

The canonical real-world use is enforcing invariants on a field. Instead of repeating the same `if value < 0: raise` check in every setter, you write the rule once as a descriptor and reuse it:

```python
class Positive:
    def __set_name__(self, owner, name):
        self.private = "_" + name          # unique per-attribute key
    def __get__(self, obj, objtype=None):
        if obj is None:
            return self
        return obj.__dict__[self.private]
    def __set__(self, obj, value):         # __set__ => DATA descriptor
        if value <= 0:
            raise ValueError("must be positive")
        obj.__dict__[self.private] = value

class Order:
    price = Positive()
    qty   = Positive()
```

`__set_name__` is called automatically at class-creation time, telling the descriptor the attribute name it was assigned to — so one `Positive` class can guard `price`, `qty`, or anything else, each storing into a distinct slot of the instance's `__dict__`. Because it defines `__set__`, it's a data descriptor: it intercepts *every* assignment to `order.price`, validates, and only then stores. There is no way around it from the instance — exactly the guarantee you want.

Once you see the protocol, the rest of Python clicks: properties, methods, slots, and ORMs are all the same four lines wearing different hats.

right.label: Non-data descriptor (only __get__): instance __dict__ WINS over it

description.en: Lookup order is data descriptor -> instance __dict__ -> non-data descriptor -> AttributeError. That is why a @property can't be shadowed by an instance attribute, but a method can.

animation_sequence.code:
class Order:
    price = Positive()

o = Order()
o.price = 19
print(o.price)
o.price = -5

variables.Order.price: Positive() (a DATA descriptor on the class)

executionSteps.description: price lives on the CLASS, not the instance. __set_name__ recorded name='price'.
Assignment is intercepted by __set__: 19 > 0 passes, stored in o.__dict__['_price'].
Reading runs __get__, returning the stored value.
A data descriptor intercepts EVERY write — invalid values never reach storage.

output: Descriptor = object with __get__/__set__/__delete__, stored on a CLASS.
__set__ present => DATA descriptor => beats the instance __dict__.
@property, methods, classmethod, staticmethod, __slots__ are ALL descriptors.

instruction.en: Implement a descriptor. `Positive` is a DATA descriptor (it defines __set__) that guards a numeric attribute: writing a non-positive value (<= 0) must raise ValueError, and a valid value must be stored per-instance. __set_name__ already records a unique private key for you. Complete __get__ (return the stored value from obj.__dict__[self.private]; return self when obj is None) and __set__ (raise ValueError if value <= 0, else store into obj.__dict__[self.private]). The demo creates a Product with a valid price and prints it. Expected output: price=19

practice_challenges.starter_code:
class Positive:
    """A DATA descriptor: defines __set__, so it beats the instance __dict__
    and intercepts EVERY assignment to the attribute it guards."""

    def __set_name__(self, owner, name):
        # Called automatically at class creation. Pick a unique private key
        # so each guarded attribute stores in its own slot of obj.__dict__.
        self.name = name
        self.private = "_pos_" + name

    def __get__(self, obj, objtype=None):
        # Accessing via the CLASS (obj is None) should return the descriptor.
        if obj is None:
            return self
        # TODO: return the value stored for this instance.
        return None

    def __set__(self, obj, value):
        # TODO: reject non-positive values with ValueError(...),
        # otherwise store value under self.private in obj.__dict__.
        pass


class Product:
    price = Positive()          # a class attribute => the descriptor is active

    def __init__(self, price):
        self.price = price       # goes through Positive.__set__


# --- demo (do not change) ---
p = Product(19)
print(f"price={p.price}")

en: A descriptor only works when stored as a CLASS attribute (Product.price = Positive()). Accessing p.price then triggers __get__/__set__ instead of returning the descriptor object.
__get__ should read the per-instance value: return obj.__dict__[self.private]. Keep the `if obj is None: return self` line so class-level access still works.
__set__ must validate first: if value <= 0: raise ValueError(f"{self.name} must be positive"). Only when it passes do you store: obj.__dict__[self.private] = value.
Because __set__ is defined, Positive is a DATA descriptor — it intercepts every assignment, so an invalid price never gets stored. The valid Product(19) prints price=19.

practice_challenges.test_code:
ok = Product(50)
assert ok.price == 50, f"valid set should store 50, got {ok.price}"
assert Product(19).price == 19
raised_neg = False
try:
    Product(-5)
except ValueError:
    raised_neg = True
assert raised_neg, "a negative price must raise ValueError"
raised_zero = False
try:
    Product(0)
except ValueError:
    raised_zero = True
assert raised_zero, "zero must raise ValueError (non-positive)"
assert isinstance(type(Product).__mro__, tuple)
assert isinstance(Product.__dict__['price'], Positive), "price must be a class-level descriptor"

q.en: What makes an object a descriptor in Python?

en: It must be created inside a function
It defines at least one of __get__, __set__, or __delete__ and is stored as a class attribute
It inherits from a special Descriptor base class
It has a __dict__ attribute

q.en: What distinguishes a DATA descriptor from a NON-DATA descriptor, and why does it matter?

en: Data descriptors are faster; it doesn't affect lookup
A data descriptor defines __set__ and/or __delete__, so it takes precedence OVER the instance __dict__; a non-data descriptor only defines __get__ and is overridden BY the instance __dict__
Data descriptors can only hold numbers
Non-data descriptors cannot be used on classes

q.en: When you evaluate obj.attr, in what order does CPython resolve it?

en: instance __dict__ -> class -> AttributeError, ignoring descriptors
data descriptor on type(obj).__mro__ -> instance __dict__ -> non-data descriptor -> AttributeError
Always the instance __dict__ first, descriptors never run
Randomly, depending on memory layout

q.en: Which Python features are built on the descriptor protocol?

en: Only @property
Only __slots__
@property, bound methods, classmethod, staticmethod, and __slots__ are all descriptors
None of them — descriptors are unrelated to those features
